exemplar.title:
To what extent did Keringâs controversial ad campaign depicting child
abuse in 2022 affect their brand image?

To what extent did Keringâs controversial ad campaign depicting child

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear identification and definition of Ethics as the key concept from the outset
Repeated reference to ethical lapses (child-exploitation images, blame-shifting, apology delays) to frame analysis
Strong linkage of ethical considerations to stakeholder reactions and CSR initiatives
weaknesses: Ethical theory (e.g., utilitarianism, Kantian duty) not fully integrated to deepen analysis
Ethics lens not woven through every paragraphâsome analytic sections drift into generic crisis-management discussion
Connection to the organization is strong but not sustained in all parts of the IA
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the student demonstrated a strong understanding of Ethics (band-4) and related it clearly to Balenciagaâs crisis, the absence of a deeper ethical framework and uneven integration across all analytical sections justify a score of 3/5.

strengths: Most sources focus on the scandal and provide both qualitative and limited quantitative insights
Student reflects on source limitations, showing conscious use of the documents
weaknesses: Bibliography lists more than five documents, which falls into the lowest band for number of documents
Lacks peer-reviewed or primary-data sources to deepen the investigation
Key data points (e.g., 19% revenue drop) lack inline citations to reinforce credibility
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Despite adequate depth in some sources (band-3), the submission provides too many documents (more than five) and fails to integrate peer-reviewed or primary documents, placing it in the 1/4 band overall.

strengths: Application of a stakeholder-mapping matrix to frame discussion
Use of an ethical lens to guide interpretation of stakeholder reactions
weaknesses: No classic business tools (SWOT, PEST, financial ratios) to complement the chosen matrix
Application remains superficialâaxes scales and data sources are unexplained
Lacks grounding in formal crisis-management or CSR theories
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The IA selects some tools (band-2) but applies them only superficially without theoretical grounding or detailed explanation, meriting 1/4.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Effective use of quantitative data (19% revenue drop, stock-price timeline) in stakeholder analysis
Self-reflection on data limitations increases transparency
Analytical links drawn between ethical breach and consumer trust erosion
weaknesses: Stakeholder-mapping diagram lacks axis criteria and source justification
Arguments occasionally asserted without counter-arguments or deeper exploration
Some sections (e.g., digital backlash) would benefit from quantitative backing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data selection and the link to the research question are adequate (band-3), and the student produces coherent analysis, though it stops short of sustained, in-depth evaluation; thus, 3/5 is appropriate.

strengths: Conclusions logically derive from earlier points on consumer backlash and revenue decline
Recommendations align with identified ethical and transparency needs
weaknesses: Restates analysis rather than synthesizing a concise, focused summary
Lacks measurable targets or timelines in recommendations
Only partially addresses the research question without quantification of impact
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While conclusions are consistent with evidence (band-2), they remain partial and insufficiently rigorous, warranting 1/3.

strengths: Clear table of contents with structured headings and page numbers
Logical flow from introduction through to conclusion with sign-posting
weaknesses: Introduction allocates excessive space to historical background at the expense of analytical focus
Research question could more precisely define variables and time frame to guide the structure
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The IA exhibits an appropriate and coherent structure throughout (band-2), justifying the full 2/2.

strengths: Includes all required elements: title page, word count, in-text citations, reference list
weaknesses: Citation formatting is inconsistent (missing italics, dates)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although all required presentation elements are present, significant inconsistencies in formatting and broken links limit clarity, placing it at 1/2.

strandSelections.reasoning: A clear table of contents, logical headings and sign-posted sections give the IA coherent flow from introduction to conclusion, satisfying the âappropriate structure throughoutâ description.

Most sources are directly related to the scandal (industry articles, finance site, marketing think-pieces) and supply both qualitative opinion and limited quantitative data, giving adequate depth for the investigation. The student also reflects on their limitations, indicating conscious use. Still, there is no truly wide range (e.g., academic journals, primary interviews) and depth of financial data is acknowledged as thin, so the work fits ârelevant and sufficiently in-depthâ rather than the top band.

The submission includes a title page with word count, a structured body, citations and a reference list, aligning with IB presentation requirements. No required element appears missing, so the highest band for presentation is appropriate.

The bibliography lists at least seven sources (Bold X Collective, Unity Marketing, RFI Asia, Business & Financial Times, Vanguard Think Tank, MVC Magazine, Yahoo Finance). Because the rubric specifies that providing more than five documents falls in the same band as supplying only one-two, the submission matches the 1-mark band for ânumber of documentsâ.

The stakeholder grid and ethical lens are applied to discuss reactions and brand image, establishing superficial relevance to the research question. However, calculations, comparative metrics or systematic application of the mapping results are minimal, so the application remains limited rather than adequate or effective.

Data use goes beyond anecdote: the student cites a 19 % revenue drop, stock-price timeline and survey results. While not comprehensive, these choices are appropriate and integrated into the narrative, meeting the âadequate selection and useâ descriptor.

The analysis links ethics to consumer trust, crisis response and financial impact, showing more than a descriptive approach. Still, arguments are occasionally asserted without deep exploration, and counter-arguments are absent. This demonstrates âsome analysis of the research question,â aligning with the 3-mark band.

The student explicitly states how the scandal affected brand image and what Kering should prioritise, offering a partial but not exhaustive answer to the research question (the extent of impact is noted qualitatively, with little quantification). This fits the descriptor âpartial answer to research question.â

Tool selection is restricted mainly to a stakeholder mapping matrix and general ethical/crisis-management concepts. Classic BM analytical tools (SWOT, PEST, Ansoff, financial ratios) are absent. This represents âsome tools and theories selected,â consistent with the 2-mark band.

Conclusions (e.g., importance of aligning marketing with societal values, need for transparency) stem logically from earlier points about consumer backlash and revenue decline, making them broadly consistent with evidence. Nevertheless, the limited data base and lack of triangulation mean the evidential link is not fully rigorous, matching the middle band.

Connection to the organisation is clear: the paper chronicles Balenciagaâs history, ownership by Kering, quantitative effects (-19 % revenue, share-price trend) and qualitative stakeholder reactions. Analysis links each ethical issue back to the companyâs brand image and crisis response across several sections. Nevertheless, some parts (e.g., generic definition of crisis management) drift from the specific firm and the integration is not sustained âthroughoutâ the IA, so the work meets the descriptor for a strong but only partially integrated connection.

The student explicitly chooses Ethics as the key concept, defines it and repeatedly relates ethical lapses (child-exploitation images, shifting blame, delayed apology) to stakeholder responses, crisis management and CSR initiatives. Ethical vocabulary is used in the introduction, analysis sections and the conclusion, showing more than a simple mention. However, ethical theory is not woven through every paragraph and there is no deeper ethical framework (e.g., utilitarianism, Kantian duty) to drive all analytical points, so full âthroughout integrationâ is not achieved, placing the work solidly in the âstrong understanding with partially integrated analysisâ band.

annotations.comment: A clear table of contents with structured headings and page numbers guides the reader effectively through the IAâs organization.
The detailed TOC entries demonstrate consistent structure and logical flow, supporting reader navigation throughout the document.
The introduction offers rich historical context but allocates excessive space to brand origin, which detracts from focus on the research questionâs ethical dimension.
The 19% revenue drop mentioned in the introduction lacks an inline citation; include a specific source reference to reinforce data credibility.
The methodology section states a mixed-methods approach but omits details on sample size, selection criteria, and survey reliability, limiting reproducibility.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The research question clearly identifies the scandalâs scope and its effect on brand image, but could benefit from explicitly defining the variables and precise time-frame to strengthen focus and guide analysis.
The student correctly identifies Ethics as the key concept at the outset, establishing coherence in the investigationâs analytical lens.
Mentioning ecological and childcare partnerships is good, but concrete examples (program names, budgets) would strengthen CSR evaluation.
The limitations section offers clear self-reflection on data sources and acknowledges biases, enhancing transparency.
Reference to Yahoo Finance stock data should specify dates or statistical measures used to compare pre- and post-scandal performance.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Key terms are defined but lack linkage to established theoretical models; for instance, apply Coombsâ Situational Crisis Communication Theory when discussing crisis management.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The stakeholder mapping diagram is presented without explanation of axis scales or data sources; include criteria for power and interest classification.
Clarify how stakeholders were plotted in each quadrant by detailing thresholds or stakeholder interview data used.
The general public section cites digital backlash but provides no quantitative support; include survey figures or media-reach statistics.
The âCustomersâ segment effectively notes social media activism; consider adding metrics on boycott rates or sentiment scores to substantiate impact.
Government/regulators discussion correctly identifies potential power, but should cite real regulatory statements or policy changes following the scandal.
Linking a 19% revenue drop to shareholder concern is well done; this effectively integrates quantitative data into stakeholder analysis.
The analysis of consumer reactions links ethical breach to trust erosion, but would be strengthened by exploring countervailing opinions from loyal customer subsets.
Qualitative citations from Unity Marketing inform the argument but their potential bias is not addressed; discuss source limitations explicitly.
A sentence fragment appears at the end of the crisis management section, disrupting flow; revise for clarity and complete the thought.
Linking the decision to sue the production company with ethical deflection strategy demonstrates analytical depth by relating actions to stakeholder perceptions.
Consider applying a formal crisis framework (e.g., SCCT) to structure the discussion of response strategies and their ethical implications.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The CSR discussion connects stakeholder expectations to ethical accountability, but lacks theoretical grounding; integrate Carrollâs CSR pyramid for rigor.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: To deepen document relevance, include peer-reviewed journal articles or primary interviews rather than relying solely on industry think-tanks.
The conclusion reiterates analysis points without providing a concise summary that directly answers the research question; refine to emphasize key findings.
Final recommendations lack measurable targets and timelines; articulate specific, time-bound actions to monitor ethical improvements.
The citation entries display inconsistent formatting (e.g., missing italics, publication dates) and incomplete URLs; standardize using an academic style guide.
The Business & Financial Times URL is truncated across lines, hindering source verification; ensure complete, accessible links.
